retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Tongue thrust
retrieved_docs.content: Children with a tongue thrust habit exert increased outward force through the tongue musculature, which can alter the eruption pathway of maxillary incisors. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Thumb Sucking -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Dental and orofacial consultations are key parts of the team management of thumb sucking.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of the tongue function together to permit a variety of shapes and functions to facilitate the production of speech and aid in the process of swallowing and manipulation of food before ingestion. The tongue must elevate posteriorly to push the bolus posteroinferiorly towards the hypopharynx and esophagus to swallow a food bolus efficiently. There are a number of disorders of the tongue described in the literature. These include but are not limited to:

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Tongue -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Tongue musculature: The various functions of the tongue require the effective functioning of the extrinsic and intrinsic tongue musculature. The inferior fibers of the genioglossus muscle help in protrusion of the tongue, the middle fibers help in depressing the tongue, whereas the superior fibers of the muscle draw the tip of the tongue backward and downward.  The hyoglossus muscle depresses and retracts the tongue, whereas the styloglossus muscle elevates and retracts the tongue. The activation of palatoglossus muscle helps in elevating the posterior aspect of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Tongue Laceration -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is a soft, muscular organ covered in mucosal epithelium, and it can sense taste via chemoreceptors and provide standard somatic sensation (see Image. The Mouth Cavity). The muscles of the tongue are divided into intrinsic and extrinsic groups. The intrinsic muscles are anchored exclusively within the tongue and change shape by contracting, lengthening, and maneuvering the tip. There are 4 intrinsic muscles: the vertical, transverse, and superior and inferior longitudinal muscles (see Image . The Mouth and Coronal Section of Tongue). The extrinsic muscles of the tongue originate from bony structures, including the genial tubercle of the mandible, the styloid process, and the hyoid bone. Four muscles comprise this group: the genioglossus, styloglossus, hyoglossus, and palatoglossus (see Image . The Mouth and Extrinsic Muscles of the Tongue). They allow the tongue to move around, protruding, retruding, elevating, and depressing.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians should examine all patients for any movement disorders before initiating treatment with dopamine D2 receptor antagonists. Tardive dyskinesia presents clinically as stereotypical involuntary movements of the tongue, neck, and facial muscles, truncal musculature, and limbs. Buccolingual movements, including masticatory muscles, are characterized by lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, perioral movements, chewing movements, or a puffing of cheeks. Sometimes, these movements may be hard to distinguish from stereotype posturing seen in chronic psychotic patients. However, tardive dyskinesia is seen in patients with chronic exposure to dopamine D2 receptor blockade.

retrieved_docs.title: Macroglossia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is made clinically on the basis comparison with a normal looking tongue due to lack of standard size of the tongue. Infants may present with feeding difficulties, poor weight gain, and failure to thrive due to protrusion of the large tongue. They may also present with speech difficulties, misaligned, or protruded teeth in later life. Necrosis may be noted on the tip of the tongue, and ulcerations may be noted throughout the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Dystonic Reactions -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Below are descriptions of the well-defined presentations of acute dystonic reactions. [9] [10] [11] Buccolingual Crisis - trismus, risus sardonicus, dysarthria, dysphagia, grimacing, tongue protrusion. Oculogyric Crisis - spasm of the extraocular muscles, most commonly deviated upward. Torticolic Crisis - abnormal asymmetric head or neck position. Tortipelvic Crisis - abnormal contractions of the abdominal wall, hip, and pelvic musculature. Opisthotonic Crisis - characteristic flexion posturing with arching of the back Laryngeal Dystonia - dysphonia, stridor Pseudomacroglossia - patient describes the sensation of tongue swelling and protrusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The median sulcus divides the tongue centrally, beginning at the apex of the tongue tip and extending to the foramen cecum, which creates the apex of a V-shaped groove, which is called the sulcus terminalis. The papillae are tiny protuberances on the dorsal tongue, especially on the anterior two-thirds, classified into three types. The first group called filiform papillae, which are the most common papillae uniformly distributed on the dorsum. They are thin papillae 1- to 2-mm without taste buds. The second type of papillae is called pointed filiform papillae, which morphologically give a rugged texture and promote the mechanical function of licking and chewing.

retrieved_docs.title: Thumb Sucking -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nonsurgical and non-orthodontic interventions that have been studied for treatment in cases of an anterior open bite originated from thumb sucking include orofacial myofunctional therapy and stomahesive wafers. Orofacial myofunctional therapy involves a set of exercises that reeducate muscles involved in swallowing, speech, and resting posture. A study done by Huang et al. included patients from 4 to 12 years who presented with dental habits utilized stomahesive wafers as therapy: these were placed on the incisive papilla to guide the tongue to rest in that position. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Structure and Function -- Food Manipulation Functions
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue moves the food around the mouth with the help of the buccinator muscle, mixes it with saliva, and helps to separate undesired particles. [5]  It also enables the formation of the food bolus in the oral preparatory phase of swallowing. It also takes part in the oral phase of swallowing by elevating and sweeping posteriorly to propel the food bolus past the anterior tonsillar pillar, triggering the swallowing reflex.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual frenulum can be attached to different areas of the tongue and alveolus. [2] A short or thick lingual frenulum may also affect the function of the tongue. [2] The examination must demonstrate tongue mobility restriction; usually, the tongue cannot be protruded beyond the vermillion border. [2] The tongue may also show a heart-shaped deformity and dimpling. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Other Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Protruding tongue Tongue thrusting; suck/swallowing disorders Feeding problems during infancy Truncal hypotonia Prognathism Wide mouth, widely spaced teeth Frequent drooling Excessive chewing/mouthing behaviors Strabismus Hypopigmented—light skin, hair, and eye color compared with the family (seen only in the deletion cases) Hyperactive lower extremity deep tendon reflexes Wide-based gait with pronated or valgus-positioned ankles Uplifted, flexed arm position especially during walking Increased sensitivity to heat Sleep disturbances Attraction to/fascination with water Abnormal food-related behaviors Obesity (in childhood) Scoliosis [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The styloglossus muscle functions to retract and elevate the tongue in a posterior and superior fashion. Following mastication, these movements promote passage of the food bolus towards the oropharynx. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Tongue -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In infants, macroglossia is manifested by noisy breathing, drooling, and difficulty while eating. It can also result in a lisping speech. The pressure exerted by the large tongue against the mandible and teeth can produce mandibular prognathism and open bite. Cleft tongue if present can lead to difficulty while eating.  In breastfeeding infants, ankyloglossia can cause feeding problems resulting in untimely weaning. It may also lead to speech defects and dental problems.  It leads to the blanching of soft tissue during extrusion of the tongue and also exerts force on mandibular anteriorly. Moreover, it interferes in the process of tooth brushing, favoring the risk of plaque accumulation, followed by tissue inflammation and gingival recession.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Digit sucking
retrieved_docs.content: This habit affects the eruption path of maxillary incisors, causing them to protrude. This occurs because the habit disrupts the balance between the outward force exerted by the tongue musculature against the palate and the inward force of the cheek musculature. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: on nose, mouth and pharynx” and to plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its relevance to head‑and‑neck and cosmetic/reconstructive surgery. Collectively, these procedures demonstrate that Excision functions as a versatile operative strategy adaptable to endocrine, hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, spinal, musculoskeletal, urologic, breast, and craniofacial contexts, supporting comprehensive patient assessment, accurate coding, and optimized multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is categorized as a placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, and the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Injection of larynx includes, as a subordinate concept, the placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway, and the broader group of other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system also contains this airway‑placement procedure. Together, these connections highlight how airway management interventions such as intubation, mechanical ventilation, and oral‑pharyngeal airway placement are organized within the taxonomy of respiratory procedures, with the larynx functioning as a central anatomical focus for both diagnostic and non‑therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التَّعَب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that patients who experience fatigue often also report significant anxious feelings. Clinically, fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي), suggesting that the presence of persistent tiredness may coincide with or signal an underlying yeast infection of the mouth. Recognizing the link between fatigue, anxiety, and oral candidiasis can aid clinicians in evaluating patients holistically, prompting assessment for both psychological distress and possible infectious contributors when fatigue is reported.
